summary:
The Peak of the Atlantic Slave Trade (c. 1700–1808 AD) represented the most intensive period of forced African migration in history — the era when the [[event:2341|Atlantic slave trade]] reached its maximum scale, transporting approximately 6-7 million enslaved Africans across the Middle Passage during the 18th century alone, with the single decade of the 1780s seeing the highest annual volume ever recorded. The peak era coincided with the explosion of Caribbean [[event:2485|sugar]] and coffee production: the island of Saint-Domingue (Haiti) alone imported approximately 30,000 enslaved people annually in the 1780s to maintain its enormous plantation output. Brazil, the largest single destination of the entire trade, imported enslaved people continuously until 1850 — receiving approximately 4.9 million of the estimated 12.5 million total Africans transported.

The peak also coincided with the rise of abolitionism. The very years of maximum slave trading — the 1780s and 1790s — were also the years when the organized abolition movement, led by Quakers and Evangelical Protestants in Britain and America, gained public traction. The British abolitionist campaign — through petitions, pamphlets, Josiah Wedgwood's famous 'Am I Not a Man and a Brother?' medallion, and Olaudah Equiano's influential autobiography (1789) — created the world's first mass humanitarian campaign. Denmark abolished the trade in 1792 ([[event:3301|effective 1803]]), Britain in 1807, the United States in 1808. The British Royal Navy's West Africa Squadron subsequently intercepted slave ships for decades — liberating approximately 150,000 enslaved people. The trade's peak and the abolition movement's rise in the same era reflects a paradox: the economic system was most profitable just as it became morally unsustainable.
